steps.title: Map the disagreement
steps.content: The students' initial reaction: the letter format is daunting and the gender-relations treatment seems old-fashioned. The professor's rebuttal: the novel uses modern-sounding language and addresses issues that still matter today.

steps.title: Predict the completion
steps.content: The professor is countering the students' surface impression that the novel is dated. The blank should capture the idea that the novel is more current/relevant than it first appears.

steps.title: Eliminate by misread/overreach
steps.content:
• Choice A — has more to say about gender relations than other novels from the same period.: introduces a comparison to other 1986 novels that the passage never makes. ✗
  • Choice B — is more relevant to contemporary audiences than it may seem at first.: matches the professor's counter that modern language and still-current issues make the novel more relevant than the students initially thought. ✓
  • Choice C — is easier to read than many contemporary novels that focus on friendship.: contradicts the students finding the format daunting and compares to contemporary novels the passage never mentions. ✗
  • Choice D — is best understood after multiple readings in different orders.: overreaches from Castillo's suggestion that letters could be read in different orders (an option) to a claim that multiple readings are required. ✗
That is the correct answer.
